coredata.dc:title: Rational design of carbon dot nanozymes for ratiometric dual-signal and smartphone-assisted visual detection of nitrite in food matrices

coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Yumin
Wang, Zihan

Dong, Wenjuan
Liu, Yang

Shuang, Shaomin

Guo, Yujing
Gong, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description: Developing reliable nitrite (NO2 −) sensors is essential for food safety and reducing health risks from NO2 − exposure. In this study, we strategically designed nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-CD) nanozymes to establish an accessible dual-signal ratiometric sensing system for detecting NO2 − in food matrices. This system utilizes the photoluminescence and enzyme-like properties of N-CD nanozymes combined with NO2 −-triggered diazotization reactions of substrates such as o-phenylenediamine (OPD) or 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). The resulting N-CD/OPD and N-CD/TMB composites provide dual-mode detection—fluorescence and colorimetric—with high selectivity for NO2 − and excellent resistance to interference. These sensors exhibit clear color changes under both ultraviolet and visible light, and can be combined with smartphones for visual, on-site detection of NO2 −. By incorporating a ratiometric strategy, dual-signal output, and smartphone compatibility, our system achieved a low detection limit (≤ 1.92 μM) and satisfactory recovery rates (85.6–115 %) in environmental water and food samples. This highlights the potential of smartphone-assisted sensors for environmental monitoring and food safety applications. Our carbon dot-based platform offers a practical and effective solution for on-site NO2 − detection, contributing valuable insights to the field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CD nanozymes
Dual-response detection
Smartphone
NO2 −
On-site

Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of NO 2
− based on the N-CD/OPD system

Ratiometric colorimetric sensing of NO 2
− based on the N-CD/TMB system

Smartphone-assisted ratiometric optical dual-modality detection of NO 2
−
Preparation and characterization of the N-CD
Photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of the N-CD

Ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on N-CD/OPD for NO 2
− detection

Ratiometric colorimetric sensor based on N-CD/TMB for NO 2
− detection

Smartphone-assisted optical dual-signal detection of NO 2
−

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Central Guidance Fund for Science and Technology Development (YDZJSX2021A004) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21705101 and 21874087). Excessive consumption of nitrite (NO2\u2212) can cause gastrointestinal problems and systemic hypoxia, making it a significant environmental toxin that requires careful monitoring. Therefore, the ability to detect NO2\u2212 on-site with high specificity, accuracy, and sensitivity is crucial. Our study presents a novel ratiometric dual-signal sensing system using nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-CD) nanozymes. N-CD nanozymes exhibit both photoluminescence and photo-responsive oxidase-like activity, coupled with NO2\u2212-induced diazotization of substrates like o-phenylenediamine (OPD) or 3,3\u2032,5,5\u2032-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). This approach integrates ratiometric sensors with dual-signal outputs into a smartphone-compatible format, significantly advancing the field of smartphone-based detection sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure for the N-CD. (b) TEM image, high-resolution TEM image (inset in the top right corner), and diameter distribution (inset in the bottom right corner) of the N-CD. (c) FT-IR spectrum, (d) high-resolution N 1 s spectrum, and (e) O 1 s spectrum of the N-CD.

(a) UV–vis absorption (red line), excitation (blue dotted line), and emission (blue solid line) spectra of the N-CD. (b) Normalized emission spectra of the N-CD under different excitation wavelengths. (c) Comparison of the photo-responsive oxidase-like activity and fluorescence property of the N-CD synthesized with different amounts of EDA.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CD, TMB, and the N-CD + TMB mixture under light irradiation. (b) Effect of pH on the oxidase-like activity of the N-CD. (c) Staircase-like behavior of oxidase-like activity when the light source is turned off and on, indicated by black and red arrows, respectively. (d) Steady-state kinetic of the N-CD measured with various concentrations of TMB. Inset: Double-reciprocal plots used to determine the kinetic constants for the TMB substrate. (e) Comparison of TMB oxidation catalyzed by the N-CD in the presence of various scavengers. (f) EPR spectra of N-CD + DMPO before and after irradiation with an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm).

(a) Schematic illustration of the construction of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for NO2 − detection, highlighting the diazotization reaction between NO2 − and OPD and the IFE between N-CD and BTA. (b) Influence of NO2 − on the fluorescence signal of the N-CD/OPD system. (c) Overlay of the emission spectrum (red line) of N-CD and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of BTA (OPD + NO2 −) (orange line). (d) Fluorescence lifetimes of the N-CD in the absence and presence of BTA. (e) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CD/OPD system with varying concentrations of NO2 −. (f) Plots of the fluorescence intensity ratio (F560/F460) of the N-CD/OPD system against NO2 − concentration. Inset: Linear relationship between F560/F460 and NO2 − concentration in the range of 0.5–250 μM. (g) Ratiometric fluorometric responses of the N-CD/OPD system to various species.

(a) Schematic illustration of the construction of ratiometric colorimetric sensors for NO2 − detection, featuring a photo-responsive oxidase-like reaction by the N-CD and a diazotization reaction between N-CD and TMBox. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-CD/TMB system and N-CD/TMB + NO2 − mixture. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-CD/TMB system and N-CD/TMB + AA mixture. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-CD/TMB system, N-CD/TMB + NO2 − mixture, and N-CD/TMB + NO2 − + AA mixture. (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-CD/TMB system after the introduction of various concentrations of NO2 −. (f) Plots of the colorimetric intensity ratio (A652/A445) of the N-CD/TMB system against NO2 − concentration. Inset: Linear relationship between A652/A445 and NO2 − concentration in the range of 0.5–100 μM. (g) Ratiometric colorimetric responses of the N-CD/TMB system to various cations and anions.

(a) Schematic diagram of the constructed ratiometric dual-signal sensing platform for the visual detection of NO2 − using a smartphone. Calibration curves obtained by plotting (b) R/B ratio and (d) R/G ratio against NO2 − concentration. Inset: Colors of (b) the N-CD/OPD system under daylight irradiation and (d) the N-CD/TMB system under ultraviolet light irradiation after adding different concentrations of NO2 −. (c) R/B ratio of the N-CD/OPD system and (e) R/G ratio of the N-CD/TMB system upon the addition of various interfering ions in the absence and presence of NO2 −.
Schematic illustration of the construction process of the ratiometric dual-signal sensing platform and its application for NO2 − detection via smartphone.
Comparison of our constructed smartphone-assisted dual-signal sensing platform with other reported smartphone sensing platforms used for NO2 − detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Ru@MOF-NH2: amino-functionalized metal-organic frameworks (MOF-NH2) integrated with [Ru(bpy)3]2+; SAM/NED: sulfanilamide (SAM)/N-(1-naphthyl)-ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (NED); B/R-CDs: bule/red emissive carbon dots; Mn/Yb/Er CeO2/TMB: Mn/Yb/Er triple-doped CeO2 nanozyme combined with 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB); Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ /TMB: manganese-doped silicon quantum dot (Mn-SiQDs)/[Ru(bpy)3]2+/TMB; N-CD/(OPD/TMB): nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-CD) nanozymes mixed with o-phenylenediamine (OPD) or TMB.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis results of NO2 − in real samples using the smartphone-assisted dual-signal sensing platform (n = 6).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
0.001＜ **
p＜0.01, 0.01＜*p＜0.05. Comparison of means obtained in fluorescence (FL) mode with those obtained in visible (Vis) mode.

head.ce:title: Rational design of carbon dot nanozymes for ratiometric dual-signal and smartphone-assisted visual detection of nitrite in food matrices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Chemistry and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Chemistry and Engineering, Yangzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Chemistry and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing reliable nitrite (NO2 −) sensors is essential for food safety and reducing health risks from NO2 − exposure. In this study, we strategically designed nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-CD) nanozymes to establish an accessible dual-signal ratiometric sensing system for detecting NO2 − in food matrices. This system utilizes the photoluminescence and enzyme-like properties of N-CD nanozymes combined with NO2 −-triggered diazotization reactions of substrates such as o-phenylenediamine (OPD) or 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). The resulting N-CD/OPD and N-CD/TMB composites provide dual-mode detection—fluorescence and colorimetric—with high selectivity for NO2 − and excellent resistance to interference. These sensors exhibit clear color changes under both ultraviolet and visible light, and can be combined with smartphones for visual, on-site detection of NO2 −. By incorporating a ratiometric strategy, dual-signal output, and smartphone compatibility, our system achieved a low detection limit (≤ 1.92 μM) and satisfactory recovery rates (85.6–115 %) in environmental water and food samples. This highlights the potential of smartphone-assisted sensors for environmental monitoring and food safety applications. Our carbon dot-based platform offers a practical and effective solution for on-site NO2 − detection, contributing valuable insights to the field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 −) is an important anion in human physiology, acting as a signaling molecule in cellular processes and serving as a biomarker for nitric oxide synthase activity [1,2]. In the food industry, NO2 − is commonly used as an additive, with both beneficial and potentially harmful effects [3]. Research has suggested a link between the consumption of processed foods and an increase risk of colorectal and stomach cancers, raising concerns about additives like NO2 − [4]. While NO2 − itself has not been directly implicated in cancer, it can react with amides or secondary amines to form carcinogenic N-nitrosamines [5]. Additionally, excessive intake of NO2 − can cause gastrointestinal problems and systemic hypoxia [6]. Thus, precise detection of NO2 − levels is crucial for maintaining food safety.

Various methods have been developed for detecting NO2 −, including chromatography [7] electrochemistry [8], and spectroscopy [9,10]. Fluorescence and colorimetric approaches are widely used for their dual-signal optical output capabilities [11,12]. However, most of these strategies rely on a single wavelength, which can limit their effectiveness due to environmental factors and variations in instrument efficiency [12]. Ratiometric sensors that utilize two wavelengths can overcome these limitations by providing intrinsic self-calibration, thereby enhancing data reliability [13]. Recent research has focused on dual-signal sensors made from composite materials that exhibit both enzyme-like activity and fluorescence. These sensors often depend on the inner filter effect (IFE) between the composite materials and oxidative products from substrates like o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) [14,15]. Typically, these optical signals are measured at a single wavelength. While single signal outputs—either fluorescence or colorimetric—can be achieved using catalytic substrates such as OPD or TMB [15,16], achieving simultaneous dual-signal output with dual wavelengths remains challenging. When NO2 − is present, it triggers a diazotization reaction that converts oxidized TMB (TMBox) into diazotized TMBox, resulting in a dual-wavelength colorimetric signal that changes with NO2 − concentration [16]. However, NO2 − detection based on the IFE between the fluorophore and TMBox has so far only been demonstrated with single-wavelength fluorescence signals [16]. Therefore, developing optical dual-signal sensors with dual-wavelength capabilities for NO2 − detection remains a significant challenge.

Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging class of carbon nanomaterials used as fluorescence probes and colorimetric nanozymes. They are popular due to their simple synthesis, low toxicity, high solubility, excellent optical properties, and tunable catalytic activities [17,18]. Given these advantages, we selected metal-free CD nanozymes to develop dual-signal, dual-wavelength sensors for NO2 − detection. One key benefit of dual-wavelength sensors is their distinct color changes, which are more easily observed by the naked eye compared to single-wavelength sensors. This makes them ideal for developing more sensitive sensing platforms, especially when integrated with smartphones [19,20]. Combining smartphone technology with optical dual-signal outputs and a ratiometric sensing strategy allows for on-site and real-time detection, significantly improving the accuracy of measurement data.

Herein, we developed a selective ratiometric dual-signal optical sensing method for visual detection of NO2 −, combined with a smartphone platform ( Scheme 1). This method utilizes nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-CD) with photoluminescence and photo-responsive oxidase-like activity, along with NO2 −-induced diazotization of substrates such as OPD and TMB. When NO2 − is introduced to the N-CD/OPD system, it forms a diazo compound, benzotriazole (BTA), resulting in an IFE between N-CD and BTA, which generates a ratiometric fluorescence response. Similarly, in the N-CD/TMB system, NO2 − converts the intermediate TMBox, formed through the catalytic activity of N-CD, into diazotized TMBox, producing a ratiometric colorimetric response that correlates with NO2 − concentration. These ratiometric sensors, based on N-CD/OPD and N-CD/TMB systems, have been successfully incorporated into a smartphone-assisted dual-signal sensing platform. This platform enables visual and semi-quantitative detection of NO2 − with excellent selectivity, demonstrating promising results in food and water samples. The method’s versatility also suggests potential for constructing similar retiometric sensing systems using other materials with both photoluminescent and enzyme-like properties. Our approach presents a novel strategy for advancing smartphone-based sensors by combining ratiometric sensors and dual-signal outputs, offering significant potential for applications in food safety and environmental monitoring.

Polyphenols, ethylenediamine (EDA), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), sodium acetate (NaAc), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), acetic acid (HAc), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), quinine sulfate (QS), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolin-N-oxide (DMPO), tryptophan (Try), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (PBQ), ascorbic acid (AA), sodium chloride (NaCl), and various metal salts were obtained from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ·cm) was utilized throughout the study.

The N-CD were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.10 g of polyphenols was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water and subjected to continuous ultrasound for 5 min. Next, varying amounts of EDA (0.5, 1.0, 1.5, or 2.0 mmol) were added to the solution. After thorough mixing, the solution was transferred to a reaction kettle and heated at 200 ºC for 8 h. Once the reaction mixture cooled naturally to room temperature, it was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane, and dialyzed using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da for 24 h. Finally, the product was freeze-dried to obtain the N-CD powder.

The morphologies and structures of the N-CD were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-1011, Japan), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, PerkinElmer, Paragon 1000, USA), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos, AXIS ULTRA DLD, Japan). Optical properties were assessed using UV–vis spectrophotometry (Varian, Cary 300, USA) and fluorescence spectrometry (Varian, Cary Eclipse, USA). Radical species were investigated through electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker, EMX-PLUS10/12, Germany), while fluorescence lifetimes were measured with a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FLS920, UK).

The relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the N-CD was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
Φ
X
=
Φ
ST
Grad

Grad
ST
η
X
2
η
ST
2
where Grad represents the gradient of the integrated fluorescence area versus absorbance, and η denotes the refractive index of the solvent. For water, the ratio η
X
/η
ST is approximately 1.0. The subscripts "ST" and "X" refer to the standard and the sample, respectively. Quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4) with a QY of 0.54 was selected as the standard to calculate the QY of the N-CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-responsive enzyme-like catalytic performances of the N-CD
ce:section.ce:para: The photo-responsive oxidase-like activity was evaluated by measuring the oxidation product of TMB (oxTMB). Specifically, 10 μL of the N-CD (10 mg/mL) and 10 μL of TMB (50 mM) were added to 980 μL of a HAc/NaAc buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 3.5). The substrate solution was then irradiated with an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm, 20 μW/cm2) for 15 min to activate the dissolved oxygen for TMB oxidation. Subsequently, the absorbance at 652 nm was recorded. Additionally, the effects of pH (ranging from 2.7 to 6.9), N-CD concentration (0.02 to 0.12 mg/mL), and irradiation time (from 2.0 to 20 min) on the oxidase-like activity were assessed using the same procedures.

The optimal conditions for TMB oxidation—pH (3.5), irradiation time (15 min), and N-CD concentrations (0.1 mg/mL)—were established prior to the dynamic kinetic study. The kinetic data, including the maximum velocity (V
max ) and Michaelis-Menten constant (K
m ), were determined using the Michaelis-Menten equation:
(2)
V =
V
max
× [ S ]
K
m
+ [ S ]

Additionally, V
max and K
m values were calculated using the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot:
(3)
1
V
=
K
m
V
m ax
×
1
[ S ]
+
1
V
max
In these equations, [S] represents the substrate concentration, and V denotes the initial reaction velocity.

Different scavengers were utilized to identify the ROS generated by the N-CD. Specifically, Try, IPA, and PBQ were introduced into each sample incubated in HAc/NaAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.5). The final concentrations of the substrates were as follows: N-CD (0.10 mg/mL), TMB (0.5 mM), Try (2.0 mg/mL), IPA (5 % v/v), and PBQ (1.0 mg/mL). The mixed solutions were illuminated with a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (20 μW/cm2) for 15 min, and the absorbance was subsequently measured using a UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer.

To further verify the presence of ∙O2 -, 10 μL of the N-CD (10 mg/mL) was injected into the HAc-NaAc buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 3.5) and irradiated with a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (20 μW/cm2) for 15 min. The mixture was then combined with DMPO (100 mM in methanol). After vortexing for 5 min, the resulting solution was analyzed using EPR.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of NO 2
− based on the N-CD/OPD system
ce:section.ce:para: A ratiometric fluorescence sensing system was constructed by combining blue-emitting N-CD and yellow-emitting benzotriazole (BTA), which is produced through the diazotization of NO2 − with OPD. Initially, varying volumes of NO2 − were sequentially added to 20 μL of OPD (50 mM) in an HCl solution (0.1 M) to facilitate BTA formation. After 5 min of diazotization, the pH of the reaction solution was adjusted to a slightly alkaline range (7.0–9.0) using 2.0 M NaOH to activate the yellow fluorescence of BTA. Subsequently, 2 μL of blue-emitting N-CD (10 mg/mL) was thoroughly mixed with the reaction solution, and ultrapure water was added to achieve a total volume of 1.0 mL. The fluorescence intensities (F560 nm/F460 nm) were measured at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm, providing ratiometric fluorescence signals for determining the linear range for NO2 − detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Ratiometric colorimetric sensing of NO 2
− based on the N-CD/TMB system
ce:section.ce:para: A ratiometric colorimetric sensing system was established based on the N-CD catalyzed oxidation of TMB oxidation and subsequent diazotization mediated by NO2 −. Under optimal conditions, 10 μL of TMB (10 mM) and 10 μL of N-CD (10 mg/mL) were sequentially added to 970 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 3.5). The mixture was exposed to ultraviolet light (365 nm, 20 μW/cm2) for 15 min, resulting in the formation of oxidized TMB (TMBox). Following this, 10 μL of NO2 - at various concentrations was introduced into the reaction solution and incubated for an additional 15 min to achieve diazotized TMBox. The absorption intensities (A652 nm/A445 nm) were then recorded, providing ratiometric colorimetric signals for determining the linear range of NO2 − detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Smartphone-assisted ratiometric optical dual-modality detection of NO 2
−
ce:section.ce:para:
Experiments for smartphone-assisted ratiometric sensing of NO2
−
using the N-CD/OPD or N-CD/TMB system were conducted in a dark chamber equipped with UV light or daylight LEDs. The N-CD/OPD or N-CD/TMB solution was incubated with NO2
−
in a tube, following previously detailed procedures. The tube was then placed inside the dark chamber, where it was irradiated by UV light (365 nm) or daylight from above, and scanned by a smartphone through a monitoring window on the chamber’s front surface. To ensure image quality and minimize background interference, the sample, light source, and smartphone were fixed in position. The smartphone utilized a WeChat mini-program called "Photo Color Recognition" to identify colors in the images and convert them into red (R), green (G), and blue (B) values. This data facilitated the establishment of a standard curve relating the R/B or R/G ratio to NO2
−
concentration. By substituting the R/B or R/G value into the standard linear equation, the concentration of NO2
−
can be effectively calculated. This setup allowed for precise and reliable measurement of NO2
−
concentration using the smartphone-assisted ratiometric sensing platform.

Cured pork, salted fish, and jerked beef samples purchased from a local supermarket were tested and pretreated according to the China National Standard (GB 5009.33–2016). The samples were blended with a sodium tetraborate solution (50 g/L) and ground into pulps using a blender. These pulps were then immersed in ultrapure water and heated at 70 ℃ for 15 min. Following this, the solution was treated with potassium ferrocyanide (106 g/L) and zinc acetate (220 g/L) to precipitate proteins, and subsequently diluted with ultrapure water to obtain a uniform solution. Water samples, including tap water and mineral water, were filtered through a 0.22 µm microfiltration membrane before detection. NO2 − standard solutions of known concentrations were then spiked into these actual samples for measurement. All measurement data were presented as mean ± standard error and analyzed using Origin 2024b (Origin Software). Statistical significance was carried out using a two-tailed unpaired t-test, with results considered significant at p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the N-CD
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-CD) was prepared by subjecting polyphenols and ethylenediamine (EDA) to a hydrothermal treatment process ( Fig. 1a). The resulting product was purified through centrifugation, filtration, and dialysis to obtain pure N-CD. The precursor ratio was optimized to fine-tune the optical and catalytic properties of the N-CD. After natural cooling to room temperature, the N-CD, exhibiting both photoluminescence and photo-responsive oxidase-like activity, was successfully synthesized. As shown in Fig. 1b, the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image demonstrates that the N-CD are well dispersed, with an average size of 3.31 ± 0.78 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in the inset of Fig. 1b highlights the high crystallinity of the N-CD, with a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the in-plane (100) plane of graphene carbon [21].

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to analyze the chemical composition of the N-CD. As shown in Fig. 1c, the FT-IR spectrum displays a vibrational peak at approximately 3390 cm−1, indicating the presence of O–H/N–H bonds on the surface of the N-CD [21,22]. Additional peaks at around 2960 and 2880 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of –CH3 and –CH2 groups, respectively [23]. Peaks observed at approximately 1570, 1480, and 1340 cm−1 are associated with CO, CC, and C–O/C–N bonds, respectively [22,23]. To further identify the functional groups present in the N-CD, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S1a) reveals an N 1 s peak appearing at 397.0 eV, confirming successful nitrogen doping in the N-CD. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S1b) shows peaks corresponding to CO (288.0 eV), C-N/C-O (286.2 eV), and C-C/C-H (285.0 eV). The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1d) is deconvoluted into graphitic N (401.2 eV) and pyridinic N (399.4 eV), while the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1e) is resolved into O-CO (535.6 eV), C-O (532.2 eV), and CO (531.0 eV) [22,24]. These findings confirm the presence of a sp2 conjugated aromatic structure with nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on the surface of the N-CD.

The fluorescence properties of the N-CD at room temperature were first examined. As shown in Fig. 2a, the N-CD exhibit two broad absorption bands at 280 nm and 340 nm, corresponding to the π→π* transition of aromatic CC bonds and the n→π* transition of surface C–O or C-N groups, respectively [25,26]. When excited at the optimal wavelength of 360 nm, the N-CD display an emission peak at 430 nm (Fig. 2a). The relative quantum yield (QY) of the N-CD aqueous solution was calculated to be 5.60 % (Fig. S2). To further explore the emission characteristics, spectra were recorded under various excitation wavelengths. As depicted in Fig. 2b, the emission spectra remain stable for shorter excitation wavelengths but show a noticeable redshift when excited above 360 nm. This excitation-dependent emission behavior is likely attributed to surface defects of the N-CD [27]. Subsequently, the relationship between emission properties and nitrogen content was investigated. Fig. 2c illustrates that the N-CD achieve their highest emission intensity when the precursor EDA content is 1.0 mmol. This finding suggests that a specific level of nitrogen doping enhances the fluorescence of the N-CD.

The fluorescence stability of the N-CD was evaluated under various conditions. As shown in Fig. S3a, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CD remained nearly unchanged after continuous ultraviolet irradiation at 365 nm for 180 min. Additionally, the N-CD exhibited stable fluorescence intensities in high-concentration KCl solutions (Fig. S3b) and across a wide range of pH values (Fig. S3c). These results demonstrate the outstanding fluorescence stability of the N-CD, highlighting their potential as robust fluorescence sensors for the analysis of complex matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of the N-CD
ce:section.ce:para: The photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of the synthesized N-CD samples was investigated using the typical oxidase substrate, TMB, in 0.20 M acetate buffer (pH 3.5) under ultraviolet light irradiation [28]. Under light irradiation, the N-CD and TMB mixture exhibited a pronounced chromogenic reaction, with an obvious absorption peak at approximately 652 nm ( Fig. 3a). This peak indicates that the N-CD catalyze the conversion of dissolved O2 to oxidize the colorless TMB substrate into blue TMBox. In contrast, without light irradiation, the mixture showed no characteristic absorption, confirming that light activation is crucial for the oxidase-like activity of the N-CD. Moreover, the catalytic activity of the N-CD increased with higher EDA content during synthesis (Fig. 2c). To achieve an optimal balance between fluorescence and oxidase-like activity, the N-CD synthesized with 1.5 mmol EDA were selected for subsequent experiments.

To optimize the oxidase-like activity of the N-CD, various reaction conditions were systematically examined, including pH, N-CD concentration, and light irradiation time. As shown in Fig. 3b, the N-CD exhibit optimal enzyme-like activity under moderately acidic conditions, with a peak performance at pH 3.5. The extent of TMB oxidation increases with higher N-CD concentrations before reaching a plateau (Fig. S4). Additionally, the N-CD display a staircase-like pattern in their oxidase-like activity when light irradiation is alternately turned on and off, indicating a light-triggered response (Fig. 3c).

The oxidase-like activity of the N-CD was further analyzed using steady-state kinetic measurements. Fig. 3d presents the typical Michaelis-Menten curves, which were converted into double reciprocal plots to determine the maximum initial velocity (Vmax) and the Michaelis-Menten constant (Km), as shown in Table S1. Compared to other reported photo-responsive oxidase-like CD nanozymes, the N-CD demonstrated a lower Km value and a higher Vmax, indicating a stronger affinity for TMB and more efficient catalysis of its oxidation on the N-CD surface. To elucidate the catalytic mechanism, reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging experiments were conducted using tryptophan (Try), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and p-benzoquinone (PBQ) as scavengers for singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radicals (∙OH), and superoxide radicals (∙O2 -), respectively. The catalytic activity of the N-CD significantly decreased upon the addition of PBQ (Fig. 3e), indicating the generation of ∙O2 - during the catalytic process. This was further confirmed by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis (Fig. 3f), which detected the formation of ∙O2 -. The proposed catalytic mechanism is as follows: upon light irradiation, the N-CD surface facilitates the decomposition of dissolved O2, generating highly active ∙O2 - radicals that rapidly oxidize TMB to its blue-colored oxidized state, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 3f.

Assessing the stability of nanozymes in complex environments is crucial for evaluating their practical applications. As displayed in Figs. S5a and S5b, the catalytic activity of the N-CD remained stable after incubation with high concentrations of KCl and after storage for 7 days. Moreover, exposure to various metallic salt solutions did not significantly impact their catalytic performance (Fig. S5c). These findings demonstrate the robust enzyme-like stability of the N-CD, making them suitable candidates for use as colorimetric sensors in complex substrates.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on N-CD/OPD for NO 2
− detection
ce:section.ce:para: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor was developed for NO2 − detection, utilizing the IFE between bule-emissive N-CD and yellow-emissive BTA. BTA is generated through the diazotization reaction of NO2 − with OPD under acidic conditions ( Fig. 4a). During this reaction, OPD reacts with NO2 − to form BTA, which contains a triazole group [29]. The pH is then adjusted to a weakly alkaline level, where BTA emits yellow fluorescence, serving as the indicator signal for NO2 - detection. Adding N-CD as a reference signal allows the creation of a ratiometric fluorescence system. As depicted in Fig. 4b, in the absence of NO2 −, the N-CD/OPD system exhibits a single fluorescence emission peak at 460 nm. Upon the addition of NO2 −, a new emission peak appears at 560 nm, resulting in dual emission peaks at 460 nm and 560 nm. This indicates the formation of BTA and its interaction with N-CD. To further explore this interaction, we employed UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The combined spectrum showed only their respective absorption peaks without the emergency of any new peaks, suggesting no significant chemical reaction between N-CD and BTA (Fig. S6). Crucially, the fluorescence intensity of N-CD at 460 nm decreases noticeably when the BTA peak at 560 nm appears, confirming an effective IFE process between N-CD and BTA. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, the absorption spectrum of BTA (OPD + NO2 −) significantly overlaps with the emission spectrum of the N-CD. To substantiate this observation, we measured the transient fluorescence spectra of the N-CD and the N-CD/BTA mixture (Fig. 4d and Table S2). The average fluorescence lifetimes were 7.18 ns for N-CD alone and 7.14 ns for the N-CD/BTA mixture, indicating the presence of IFE between N-CD and BTA [30]. The simultaneous changes in fluorescence intensity and emission color demonstrate the potential of this system as a robust bimodal ratiometric fluorescence platform for the sensitive detection of NO2 −.

Key parameters for the diazotization of OPD, including solution acidity before the reaction, diazotization time, and alkalinity after the reaction, were optimized (Fig. S7). Under these conditions, different concentrations of NO2 − were added to the N-CD/OPD system, and the fluorescence intensity ratio (F560/F460) was measured to assess the sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor. As displayed in Fig. 4e, increasing amounts of NO2 − caused a consistent decrease in emission intensity at 460 nm and a marked increase at 560 nm. The F560/F460 ratio exhibited a linear increase with NO2 − concentration (Fig. 4f), following the equation F560/F460 = 0.00141 C + 0.3535 (R2 = 0.998), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.22 μM (S/N = 3). This LOD is well below the World Health Organization’s maximum allowable concentration for NO2 − in drinking water (0.65 μM or 3.0 mg/L) [31]. In China, safe NO2 − levels in food and beverages range from 2.0 to 30 mg/kg (1.0–13 μM) [32], indicating the potential application of our N-CD/OPD sensor for monitoring NO2 − in various foods and drinks. Although NO2 − detection can be achieved using either N-CD [33] or OPD [34] alone, both approaches are limited to single-wavelength emission, making them susceptible to environmental fluctuations and instrumental inefficiencies. Our dual-wavelength N-CD/OPD system overcomes these limitations, providing a more reliable detection method.

High selectivity is essential for sensors operating in complex biological environments. To test the selectivity of the N-CD/OPD system, we recorded emission spectra after adding 1.0 mM of potential interfering substances. As depicted in Fig. 4g, the system’s ratiometric response to NO2 − was not influenced by the presence of various metal ions and other anions. Additionally, the analysis of real samples showed satisfactory spiked recoveries ranging from 95.0 % to 106 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 1.68 % and 5.13 % (Table S3). These results illustrate the sensor’s good selectivity and potential applicability for NO2 − detection in complex matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Ratiometric colorimetric sensor based on N-CD/TMB for NO 2
− detection
ce:section.ce:para: A ratiometricc colorimetric sensor for NO2 − detection was established based on N-CD-catalyzed oxidation of TMB and subsequent diazotization induced by NO2 − ( Fig. 5a). Under ultraviolet light, N-CD catalyze the conversion of dissolved O2 into highly active superoxide radicals (∙O2 -), which oxidize the colorless TMB substrate to form blue TMBox. This photo-responsive oxidase-like reaction occurs in an acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.5). Upon adding NO2 −, diazotization of TMBox occurs, resulting in the formation of green diazotized TMBox (gTMBox). The color change from blue to yellow signifies an absorption shift in the N-CD/TMB system, laying the groundwork for the ratiometric colorimetric sensor. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the N-CD/TMB system displays two absorptions peak at 652 nm and 445 nm. The intensities of these absorption change inversely with the introduction of NO2 −. It is hypothesized that NO2 − reacts with the aromatic primary amine group of TMBox, leading to the formation of diazo groups, which results in a decrease at 652 nm and an increase at 445 nm. To further investigate the diazotization process between TMBox and NO2 −, control experiments were carried out. As illustrated in Fig. 5c, N-CD oxidize TMB to form blue TMBox, which can be completely reduced back to colorless TMB by ascorbic acid (AA, 100 μM). However, in the presence of NO2 −, TMBox undergoes diazotization to produce gTMBox (Fig. 5d). Notably, AA can reduce gTMBox to yellow diazotized TMBox (yTMBox) but not back to colorless TMB. Specifically, AA can chemically reduce the semiquinone-imine structure of gTMBox to an amino structure, but it cannot reduce the diazo group (Fig. S8). This confirms that the diazotization occurs between TMBox and NO2 −. Based on these findings, the N-CD/TMB-based ratiometric colorimetric sensor demonstrates effective potential for NO2 − detection.

The sensitivity of the N-CD/TMB system as a ratiometric colorimetric sensor was assessed by optimizing the diazotization time for TMBox to 15 min (Fig. S9). Various concentrations of NO2 − were introduced into the N-CD/TMB system, and the absorbance ratio (A652/A445) was recorded. As revealed in Fig. 5e, increasing concentrations of NO2 − led to a decrease in the absorption peak at 652 nm and an increase at 445 nm. Furthermore, the A560/A445 ratio exhibited a good linear relationship with the logarithm of NO2 − concentration in the range of 0.5 to 100 μM. The linear regression equation was A652/A445 = 1.2057 C + 2.4938, with an R2 value of 0.995 (Fig. 5f). The LOD was calculated to be 0.18 μM (S/N = 3). Similar to the N-CD/OPD system, the LOD of the N-CD/TMB sensor is also below the maximum allowable concentration of NO2 − in food and beverages as set by the World Health Organization and Chinese regulations [31,32]. Other enzyme-like materials have been developed for ratiometric colorimetric detection of NO2 −, including Fe-single-atom catalysts [35], cobalt-nitrogen co-doped carbon [36], and microbe-supported Pd nanocomposites [37]. However, these materials often rely on metal ions as active catalytic components, which can be inhibited by certain anions such as F− [38], PO4 3− [14], and S2− [39]. In contrast, our N-CD is metal-free and its enzyme-like activity can be activated by ultraviolet light. This reduces the need for hydrogen peroxide, a common substrate in enzyme-like reactions, thereby minimizing interference from anions during NO2 − detection. The selectivity of the ratiometric colorimetric sensor was tested by introducing 1.0 mM of various potentially interfering analytes into the N-CD/TMB system. The results indicated that the sensor’s response to NO2 − remained unaffected by the presence of other cations and anions (Fig. 5g). Additionally, the analysis of actual samples (Table S4) demonstrated good spiked recoveries of NO2 −, ranging from 98.4 % to 106 %, with RSDs between 1.09 % and 5.04 %. These findings demonstrate that the N-CD/TMB system is highly selective and accurate for detecting NO2 − in water and food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Smartphone-assisted optical dual-signal detection of NO 2
−
ce:section.ce:para: The constructed N-CD/OPD and N-CD/TMB sensors demonstrate higher accuracy than other published N-CD-based sensors (Table S6). This improved performance is attributed to the NO2 −-induced diazotization of OPD or TMB, along with dual-signal outputs (fluorescence and absorbance) and dual-wavelength ratios (F560/F460 and A652/A445). Interestingly, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on the N-CD/OPD system and the ratiometric colorimetric sensor using the N-CD/TMB system exhibit distinct color changes in response to varying NO2 − concentrations. This feature supports the development of a smartphone-assisted optical dual-modality sensing platform for on-site and visual monitoring of NO2 −. Fig. 6a illustrates the detection process of NO2 − using this smartphone-assisted platform. The smartphone captures, recognizes, transforms, and analyzes a series of fluorescence and visible images (refer to the Experiments Section). As depicted in Figs. S10 and 6b, adding NO2 − to the N-CD/OPD system changes the fluorescence color from blue to yellow, establishing a strong linear correlation between the R/B value and NO2 − concentration (R/B = 0.0048 C + 0.3544, R2 = 0.993), where R and B represent the red and blue values of the fluorescence images, respectively. The LOD was calculated to be 1.92 μM. Similarly, introducing various NO2 − concentrations into the N-CD/TMB system results in a color change from blue to yellow, as shown in Figs. S9 and 6d. The R/G value displays a robust linear relationship with NO2 − concentration in the range of 5.0 to 100.0 µM, represented by the linear regression equation R/G = 0.0048 C + 0.5836 (R2 = 0.995), where R and G are the red and green values of the visible images, respectively. The LOD for this system was determined to be 1.36 μM. Both LODs are significantly lower than the maximum permissible intake of NO2 − in food and beverages [31,32], making the smartphone-assisted dual-signal sensing platform suitable for on-site monitoring in food matrices. Previous studies have explored sensors based on single-intensity measurements for visual NO2 − detection, relying on color changes visible to the naked eye or smartphone [12,40,41]. However, single-color variations complicate the accurate quantification of NO2 − content. Our method employs a ratiometric strategy, enabling more pronounced color changes that can be detected with greater precision by smartphones. This approach enhances detection sensitivity compared to platforms based solely on single-color changes or naked-eye recognition.

The selectivity of the smartphone-assisted optical sensing platform for NO2 − was also evaluated, paralleling instrument-assisted optical measurements. Figs. 6c and 6e demonstrate that the R/B or R/G ratios significantly increase upon the introduction of NO2 − into the N-CD/OPD or N-CD/TMB systems, remaining unaffected by interfering analytes such as metal ions and inorganic ions. This highlights the platform’s strong selectivity and anti-interference capability for NO2 − detection. When compared to other smartphone-assisted sensing platforms for NO2 − detection ( Table 1) [11,12,41–44], our platform offers advantages such as lower detection limits, a broader detection range, and higher reliability. These benefits stem from the dual-wavelength strategies that produce more noticeable color changes and independent dual-signal channels that mitigate external distractions. The dual-signal modality enhances accuracy and reliability, making it more effective for practical NO2 - detection in real samples compared to other reported platforms.

Having confirmed the excellent selectivity of the smartphone-assisted optical dual-signal sensing platform for NO2 −, we proceeded to evaluate its practicality using the standard addition method in real sample monitoring. Table 2 presents the detection results from various actual samples, including cured pork, salted fish, jerked beef, tap water, and mineral water. Initially, NO2 − was detected in the food samples but not in the water samples. After adding NO2 − at concentrations of 5 μM, 10 μM, and 30 μM to these samples, the spiked recoveries ranged from 85.6 % to 115 %, with RSDs between 4.14 % and 12.2 %. We also conducted a significant difference analysis between the means obtained using our developed methods. The statistical results indicate a significant difference between the means measured by the dual-signal ratiometric sensors (Tables S3 and S4) and those obtained using the smartphone-assisted dual-signal sensing platform (Table 2). This suggests that single-signal sensing systems may introduce measurement errors, while the dual-signal strategy enhances the accuracy of the measurement data. To further demonstrate the accuracy of the smartphone-assisted optical dual-modality sensing platform for NO2 − detection, we compared our results with those obtained using the Chinese standard method (GB 5009.33–2016) [45]. The detection results from the standard method (Table S5) showed a high level of agreement with those obtained from our proposed method (Table 2). This consistency indicates the reliability and accuracy of the smartphone-assisted optical dual-signal sensing platform for quantitatively detecting NO2 − in real samples.

In summary, we developed a ratiometric dual-signal sensing system (N-CD/OPD and N-CD/TMB) incorporated into a smartphone-based device for NO2 − analysis. This system combines selectivity, portability, and visual detection, demonstrating excellent analytical performance with high selectivity and resistance to external interference. These attributes position it as a promising tool for on-site NO2 − detection in real-world environments. The smartphone-assisted detection procedure enables semi-quantitative NO2 − analysis through color identification, making it a viable dual-signal sensing platform for real-time monitoring. The system also exhibited a low detection limit (≤ 1.92 μM) and satisfactory recovery rates (85.6 % to 115 %) in actual samples. We believe this study sets the foundation for innovative sensing strategies in practical NO2 − detection applications.

Yang Liu: Formal analysis. Chuan Dong: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Shaomin Shuang: Writing – review & editing. Xiaojuan Gong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yujing Guo: Validation, Methodology. Qin Hu: Writing – review & editing. Zihan Wang: Formal analysis. Minyu Hao: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenjuan Dong: Validation. Huiping Wang: Software, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Central Guidance Fund for Science and Technology Development (YDZJSX2021A004) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21705101 and 21874087).

ce:section.ce:para: Excessive consumption of nitrite (NO2− ) can cause gastrointestinal problems and systemic hypoxia, making it a significant environmental toxin that requires careful monitoring. Therefore, the ability to detect NO2− on-site with high specificity, accuracy, and sensitivity is crucial. Our study presents a novel ratiometric dual-signal sensing system using nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-CD) nanozymes. N-CD nanozymes exhibit both photoluminescence and photo-responsive oxidase-like activity, coupled with NO2 −-induced diazotization of substrates like o-phenylenediamine (OPD) or 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). This approach integrates ratiometric sensors with dual-signal outputs into a smartphone-compatible format, significantly advancing the field of smartphone-based detection sensors.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2024.136156.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
